Cool. The next one I'd like to talk about is regression based evaluation. And the way this works
is this is usually used in a supervised setting. So the way what you have to do is you have to
calculate a whole bunch of like actual human judgments. And usually these judgments can either be
direct assessment where you actually have a score or they can be pairwise judgments. And then
if you have direct assessment, you use a regression based loss like minimum square to error.
If you have pairwise, you use a ranking based loss that tries to upweight the ones that are
higher scoring and down with the ones that are lower scoring. One typical example of this is
comment, which is or has been at least for a very long time, the state of the art and machine
translation evaluation. And the reason why it works so well is because we have a bunch of
evaluations for machine translation. They've been doing evaluation machine translation systems
for years and you can use that as lots of supervised training data. So basically you just take
these evaluation data, you have human annotations, you have the output according to a model like
comment, you calculate the difference between them and you update model parameters.
The problem, this is great if you add lots of training data. The problem with this is for a
lot of tasks we don't have lots of training data. So training these is a little bit less feasible.
And now recently what we have been moving into is a QA based evaluation, which is basically where
we ask a language model how good the output is. And so GEMBA is an example, one of the early
examples of this for machine translation evaluation where they basically just ask a GPG-4,
like score the following translation from a source language to target language with respect
to the human reference on a continuous scale from 0 to 100 where the score of 0 means no meaning
preserved in the score of 100 means a perfect meaning in grammar. You feed in the source,
you feed in the the human reference optionally if you have a human reference and then you feed in
the target and you get a score. And so this works pretty well, this can give you better results.
There's a, especially if you have a strong language model, the problem is it's very unpredictable
whether this is going to work well and it's very dependent on the prompt that you're using.
So right now a lot of people are using GPG-4 without actually validating whether it does a good
job at evaluation and my results are all across the board. It can be anywhere from very, very good
to very, very bad at evaluating particular tasks. So I would be at least a little bit suspicious
of whether GPG-4 is doing a good job at evaluating for your task, especially more complex tasks.
I would especially be suspicious if you're doing two, any of the two following things.
Number one, if you're comparing GPG-4 or any model against itself in another model,
because GPG-4 really likes GPG-4. It really likes its own outputs and there are papers,
sorry, I don't actually have the references here but I can follow up with people I hope to see them.
But there are papers that demonstrate that GPG-4 likes its own outputs more than others.
Also, if you're explicitly optimizing the outputs using RLH app,
there is something called Goodheart's Law which is basically any time you start optimizing
towards a metric, it becomes a bad metric and that also happens for GPG-4 based evaluations.
So if you start optimizing for GPG-4 based evaluations, especially for reference list metrics that
don't use a reference output, then you start basically exploiting the metric.
Another thing that you can do with QA-based evaluation is ask about fine-grade mistakes.

And basically what we did is we asked the model to not give a particular score,
but actually identify the mistakes in the output. And when we asked it to identify the mistakes
in the output, we found that there's more consistent results.
